problem: Let F(n) be the maximum possible size of a subset A⊆\{1,\ldots,n\} such that a\nmid bc whenever a,b,c∈ A with a≠ b and a≠ c. Is there a constant C such thatF(n)=\pi(n)+(C+o(1))n^{2/3}(\log n)^{-2}?
original_latex:
Let $F(n)$ be the maximum possible size of a subset $A\subseteq\{1,\ldots,n\}$ such that $a
mid bc$ whenever $a,b,c\in A$ with $a
eq b$ and $a
eq c$. Is there a constant $C$ such that\[F(n)=\pi(n)+(C+o(1))n^{2/3}(\log n)^{-2}?\]
additional_context:
Erdős \cite{Er38} proved there exist constants 0<c_1≤ c_2 such that\pi(n)+c_1n^{2/3}(\log n)^{-2}≤ F(n) ≤ \pi(n)+c_2n^{2/3}(\log n)^{-2}.Erdős \cite{Er69} gave a simple proof that F(n) ≤ \pi(n)+n^{2/3}: define a graph with vertex set the union of those integers in [1,n^{2/3}] with all primes p∈ (n^{2/3},n]. We have an edge u\sim v if and only if uv∈ A. It is easy to see that every m≤ n can be written as uv where u≤ n^{2/3} and v is either prime or ≤ n^{2/3}, and hence there are ≥ | A| many edges. This graph contains no path of length 3 and hence must be a tree and have fewer edges than vertices, and we are done. This can be improved to give the upper bound mentioned by using a subset of integers in [1,n^{2/3}].

More generally, one can ask for such an asymptotic for the size of sets such that no a∈ A divides the product of r distinct other elements of A, with the exponent 2/3 replaced by (2)/(r+1).

For further discussion and references concerning this generalisation to r≥ 3 see the comment by Wouter van Doorn.

See also [425].

References

[Er38] P. Erdős, On sequences of integers no one of which divides the product of two others and on related problems. Tomsk. Gos. Univ. Ucen Zap. (1938), 74-82.

[Er69] Erdős, Paul, Some applications of graph theory to number theory. The Many Facets of Graph Theory (Proc. Conf.,
Western Mich. Univ., Kalamazoo, Mich., 1968) (1969), 77-82.
reference_proof_hint:
This is essentially Erdős’s “property (P_2)” extremal problem [[nomath]](sometimes phrased as the maximum size of a $2$-primitive subset of $[n]$)[[/nomath]], where one forbids a divisibility relation (a_0\mid a_1a_2) among (distinct) elements. In the standard formulation one requires (a_0,a_1,a_2) to be *all distinct*; your condition is slightly stronger because you also forbid the case $b=c$ [[nomath]](since you require only $a\neq b$ and $a\neq c$)[[/nomath]]. In particular, any set satisfying your condition also satisfies the standard (P_2) condition, so your $F(n)$ is (\le) the usual extremal function.

What is known is that the extremal size has main term (\pi(n)), and the best general results pin down the “error term” only up to *constant factors*:
[
F(n)=\pi(n)+\Theta\left(\frac{n^{2/3}}{(\log n)^2}\right),
]
in the standard (P_2) setting. This is explicitly summarized in later work: Erdős determined that the maximum size is (\pi(n)+\Theta(n^{2/3}/(\log n)^2)) (i.e., he got the
